← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Southeast Asia & Oceania

Temple cities, island stone, and the deepest-time painting tradition on earth.

From the sandstone temple-mountains of Angkor and Borobudur to basalt moai on Easter Island and rock shelters in northern Australia where painting has gone on for 20,000 years.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Uluru
Aṉangu · sacred site · Australia
A sandstone monolith rising 1,100 feet out of the center of the continent. For the Aṉangu it is the physical record of ancestral beings' creation journeys.
Indigenous Australian Art
Aboriginal · millennia to present · Australia
Bradshaw paintings (Gwion Gwion) in the Kimberley, possibly 20,000 years old — elongated figures painted with pigment that has bonded permanently with the rock surface.
Tazza
South East Asia, Thailand, Ban Chiang, Neolithic period · 3000s BCE · reddish earthenware with painted and incised decoration

1000 BCE – 0

Wandjina Rock Art
Kimberley · 3000+ years old · Australia
White face with huge dark eyes and no mouth, rayed head like a halo. Wandjina are the creator spirits of the western Kimberley, repainted by traditional custodians over generations.
Male Head
South East Asia, Thailand, Ban Chiang, Neolithic period · c. 1000 BCE · bronze
Manunggul Jar
Late Neolithic · c. 890–710 BCE · Palawan, Philippines
A secondary burial jar whose lid is crowned by two figures in a soul boat: one dead, one paddling, arms crossed, eyes closed. Among the most important prehistoric objects in Southeast Asia, it appears on the Philippine 1,000-peso note.
Đông Sơn Culture
Bronze Age · 700 BCE – 100 CE · Vietnam
Bronze drums cast with spiral patterns and concentric bird-men. Traded across mainland Southeast Asia — a pre-Indic shared world.
Ban Chiang
Bronze Age · 1500 BCE – 300 CE · Thailand
Painted red-on-buff pottery from a prehistoric village in northeast Thailand — swirling spirals and concentric whorls. Bronze was cast here earlier than the textbooks once allowed.
Plain of Jars
Iron Age · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Laos
Thousands of carved stone jars scattered across a plateau. Probably funerary; ground-truth research has been slow because the plain is still littered with unexploded American bombs.
Moon of Pejeng
Dong Son tradition · c. 300 BCE · Bali
The largest bronze kettledrum in the world — almost two meters across. In Balinese legend it's the fallen wheel of a chariot of the moon.

0 – 500 CE

Ubirr Rock Art
Aboriginal Australian · up to 20,000 years old · Kakadu
One of the oldest continuous art traditions on earth. Later paintings show fish anatomy so precisely that species are still identifiable.
Foliage Relief
Cambodia · Mid 11th century
Statuette
Cambodia · 8th century
Head
Cambodia · 10th century-11th century
Standing Krishna
Cambodia · 6th century
Sculpture
Cambodia · 12th century
Standing Visnu
Cambodia · 11th century
Head of the Buddha
Ayutthaya · 17th century
Standing Buddha
Thailand · 15th century-16th century
Banner
Thailand · mid 19th century
Head of Buddha
Lopburi, Thailand · 13th century
Painting
Thailand · mid 19th century
Parvati
East Java · 15th century
Seated Figure of a Bodhisattva
Java · late 9th century - early 10th century
Seated Buddha
Central Java · 9th century-10th century
Buddha Sakyamuni
Java · 10th century - 11th century
Figure
Burma · 19th century
Box
Thailand · late 19th century
Jar
Vietnam · 15th century-16th century
Ceremonial Shoulder Cloth (Kain songket lepus)
by Malay
Indonesia · indonesian · Two widths joined: silk and silvered-and gilt-metal threads wrapped cotton
Bell-handle
Cambodia · 12th century - 13th Century
Finial
Cambodia · 12th century-13th century
Plaque
Thailand
Tara
Java · Late 9th century - early 10th century
Bodhisattva Avalokitesvara
Java · ca. 11th century
Victoria 'Queen' of the Nukumaru
by Peyman, Benjamin
New Zealand · ca. 1860s
Panel
Philippines · 19th century
Collar
Philippines · 19th century
Ewer
Vietnam · 15th century
Bottle
Vietnam · late 15th century-16th century
Bark cloth
Fiji · 19th century
Relief with Kala Mask
Java · ca. 15th century
Mirror
East Java · ca. 15th century
Cattle Bell
East Java · ca. 15th century
Relief Panel
Java · 14th century
Relief
Java · 8th century
Agastya
Java · ca. 10th century
SHERD
Sukhothai · 13th century
Box and lid
Thailand · 15th century-16th century
Jar and lid
Thailand · 15th century
Kiln support
Thailand · ca. 15th century-16th century
Burmese manuscript
Burma · Probably 19th century
Manuscript and binding boards
Burma · late 18th century-early 19th century
Manuscript
Burma · late 19th century
Hanging
Burma · late 19th century
Bodhisattva, probably Maitreya, seated in Royal Ease.
Burma · 8-9th century
Votive tablet
Pagan · 11th century-12th century
Brahma
Burma · 11th century
Buddhist votive tablet
Pagan · 11th century - 12th century
Fragment
Pagan · 11th century
Table
by Hsaya Khant
Pagan · 1st half 20th century
Buddha Sakyamuni Preaching
Thailand · 7th- 8th century
Dharmachakra 'Wheel of Law'
Dvaravati kingdom, Thailand · 7th century - 8th century
Sarong
Sumatra · 19th century
Shoulder cloth (ulos)
Sumatra · 20th century
Jacket
Indonesia · late 19th century - early 20th century
Cover
Samoa · 19th century

500 – 1000

Upper stele with hand for Krishna Lifting Mount Govardhan
Southern Cambodia, Takeo Province, Phnom Da · c. 600 · Sandstone
Krishna Lifting Mount Govardhan (Phnom Da)
Pre-Angkor Khmer · c. 600 · Cambodia
A colossal sandstone Krishna from Phnom Da — arm raised to hold a mountain that is no longer there, its lost upper half preserved only in a second stele fragment.
Buddha with Hands in Gesture of Teaching, Vitarka Mudra
Thailand, Mon-Dvaravati period · 600s · bronze
Feet for Standing Buddha (1973.15)
Thailand, Mon-Dvaravati Period, c. 7th Century · c. 600s · sandstone
Plaque with Vishnu
Thailand, Sri Deb Style, Mon-Dvaravati period, c. 7th-8th Century · c. 600s · gold repoussé
Seated Bodhisattva Maitreya
by Burma
Myanmar · Southeast Asian · 601–700 · Bronze
Buddha with Attendants on Mythical Animal
by Thailand, Lopburi province
Thailand · Southeast Asian · 601–800 · Sandstone
Sepik Clan Mask
Middle Sepik · c. 700 CE · Papua New Guinea
A carbon-dated wooden mask 1,200 years older than European contact, showing the visual tradition of the Sepik was continuous long before colonial ethnography "discovered" it.
Cambodian Avalokiteshvara (Pre-Angkor)
Pre-Angkor Khmer · 7th–8th c. · Cambodia
A standing sandstone bodhisattva from western Cambodia, pre-Angkor and pre-Hindu revival — one of the earliest Southeast Asian Buddhist sculptures, carved when Indic religions were freshly arrived.
Dvaravati Buddha Bust
Dvaravati · c. 700 CE · Thailand
A limestone bust with heavy lidded eyes — the Mon-Dvaravati style of central Thailand, one of Southeast Asia's earliest Buddhist sculptural traditions.
Padmapani
Java, Pagan, Sailendra Period, 8th-9th Century · 700s-800s · copper
Bracket with Buddha and a Pair of Acolytes
Thailand, Mon-Dvaravati style, probably from Nagara Pathama, 8th-9th Century · 700s–800s · gray schist
Shwedagon Pagoda
Mon → Burmese · 6th–11th c. onward · Yangon
A gilded stupa 325 feet tall, covered in gold leaf and crowned with 4,000 diamonds. Said to enshrine hairs of the Buddha.
Goddess
Indonesia, Central Java, Boyolali · 800s · volcanic stone
Seated Avalokitesvara
Central Java, 9th century · 800s · bronze
Avalokiteshvara (Bodhisattva of Mercy)
Java · 800s · bronze
Head of a "Bearded Man"
Central Java, Borobudur, c. 9th Century · c. 800s · lava stone
Head of a Female Deity
by Indonesia
Borneo · Southeast Asian · 801–1300 · Sandstone
Borobudur Buddha Head
Sailendra · 9th c. · Java
A volcanic-stone head once attached to one of Borobudur's 504 seated Buddhas, broken off and removed in the colonial period. Quiet, unflinching, the eyes half-closed.
Candi Mendut
Sailendra · 9th c. · Java
A Buddhist temple 3 km from Borobudur, squat under a great stepped roof. Inside, three monumental stone Buddhas — among the finest Javanese Buddhist sculptures surviving intact.
Mahakala Door Guardian
Sailendra · 9th c. · Central Java
A stocky volcanic-stone guardian meant to flank a temple doorway, club raised, belly exposed in defiance. Mahakala guards a threshold still, in a gallery, against forgetfulness.
Borobudur
Sailendra dynasty · 9th c. · Java, Indonesia
A stone mandala you walk. Climbing its nine terraces takes you through 2,672 relief panels and 504 Buddhas to the empty stupa at the top.
Prambanan
Medang Kingdom · 9th c. · Java, Indonesia
The Hindu counterpart to Borobudur a few miles away — a cluster of tall, sharp-edged temples to the Trimurti, walls carved with the Ramayana.
Mỹ Sơn
Champa Kingdom · 4th–14th c. · Vietnam
Brick Hindu temples in a jungle valley. Seventy stood before American bombs in 1969; twenty survive, still slowly giving up their secrets.
Wayang Kulit
Java · 9th c. onward · Indonesia
Flat leather shadow puppets of the Ramayana and Mahabharata, manipulated against a backlit screen. A traditional performance runs all night.
Balinese Temples
Balinese Hindu · 9th c. onward · Bali
Every village has at least three: for the ancestors, for daily life, for the dead. Split gates, black palm-fiber roofs, statues wrapped in checkered cloth.
Champa Enthroned Deity
Champa · c. 850 CE · Vietnam
A seated stone planetary deity — one of nine graha — from a 9th-century Cham temple at Quang Binh. Indian astrological theology carved on the central Vietnamese coast.
Pyu Standing Buddha
Burma · Pyu period · 9th c. · kaolin
A small kaolin-clay Buddha made for a Pyu-period shrine in pre-Pagan Burma — before the Bamar conquest and the Theravada consolidation under King Anawrahta in the 11th century.
A Goddess
by Vietnam
Vietnam · Southeast Asian · 850–950 · Bronze
Lintel with Garuda
Cambodia · 875–925 · sandstone
Po Nagar
Champa · 7th–12th c. · Vietnam
Cham brick towers on a hill above the Nha Trang river, dedicated to the mother-goddess Yan Po Nagar. Still active — Vietnamese and Cham Hindus both bring offerings.
Head of a Follower of Shiva
Cambodia, Angkor, 10th century · 900s · sandstone
Bell with Vajra Handle
Indonesia, Java, 10th century · 900s · bronze
Bodhisattva Padmapani
Java, predating Nganjuk style, 10th century · 900s · bronze
Elephant
Vietnam, Tra-Kieu, 1st half of 10th Century · 900–950 · sandstone
Prambanan Triad
Sailendra · early 10th c. · Central Java
Three seated deities from the great Hindu temple complex of Loro Jonggrang at Prambanan — the brief Javanese reassertion of Shiva worship between waves of Buddhism.
Head of a Deity or a Deified King
Cambodia, Koh Ker, Reign of Jayavarman IV, 928-941 · mid-900s · sandstone
Preah Vihear
Khmer Empire · 9th–11th c. · Cambodia
A cliff-top temple on the Thai border — 800 meters of causeway climbing to a sanctuary looking out over the plain. Thailand and Cambodia fought over its ownership in 2011.
Champa Heraldic Lion
Champa · 10th c. · Vietnam
A sandstone lion from a Cham temple — paws and ears stylized, facing forward, closer to a Hindu Indian lion than to the actual animal. Cham architecture is Hinduized Vietnam.
Champa Apsara Bracket
Champa · 10th c. · Vietnam
A sandstone apsara — a celestial dancer — once set as a bracket beneath a Cham temple roof. Her hip cocked, her breasts heavy, her expression far away.
Khmer Durga Slaying the Buffalo Demon
Khmer · Koh Ker · 10th c. · bronze
Durga pressing her trident into the buffalo-demon Mahishasura — cast at Koh Ker, the sandstone capital Jayavarman IV built when he split from Angkor for seventeen years before the court returned.
Banteay Srei
Khmer Empire · 967 · Cambodia
"Citadel of Women" — a small temple of pink sandstone carved with almost impossibly fine reliefs. Malraux tried to steal four figures in 1923 and was arrested.

1000 – 1500

Javanese Bronze Deity (Female)
Central Javanese · 10th–11th c.
A seated female deity holding a conch — part of the Ngandjuk bronze find, a cache of 94 ritual figures buried in Java when Mataram's power faltered.
Javanese Bronze Deity (Vajra)
Central Javanese · 10th–11th c.
A male deity supporting a vajra on his finger — from the same Ngandjuk cache, showing the specifically Buddhist-Tantric side of late Mataram devotion.
Yakshini's Head
Java, Eastern Javanese Period · c. 11th Century · terracotta
Bust of a Female Deity (Devi)
by Cambodia
Cambodia · Southeast Asian · 1001–1100 · Sandstone
Baphuon Kneeling Male Figure
Khmer · Baphuon period · 11th c. · bronze
A bronze figure kneeling in añjali, the gesture of adoration — a portrait of a minister or king. Baphuon-period metalwork carried Khmer bronze to its height just before Angkor Wat.
Standing Female Figure
Cambodia, Angkor, Baphuon, Angkorean Period (877-1431) · 1050–75 · sandstone
Celestial Beauty (<em>Apsara</em>) in Adoration
by Vietnam, probably Thu Thien, Binh Dinh province
Vietnam · Southeast Asian · 1075–1125 · Sandstone
Shamvara and A Dakini
Cambodia, Angkor, 11th century · c. 1100 · bronze
Shamvara
Cambodia, Angkor, 11th century · c. 1100 · bronze
Golden Rock
Burmese Buddhist pilgrimage · Kyaiktiyo · Myanmar
A boulder entirely covered in gold leaf, perched on a cliff edge. Legend says it balances on a single strand of the Buddha's hair; male pilgrims apply new leaf, women may only look from below.
Phnom Rung
Khmer · 10th–13th c. · Thailand
A sandstone Khmer temple on an extinct volcano, aligned so the sun rises through all fifteen doorways on a few days each year. Once lost in jungle; restored in the 1980s from 8,000 numbered fragments.
Conch Shell with a Figure of Hevajra
Cambodia, Khmer, Angkor Period, 12th-13th Century · 1100s · shell and bronze
Sheath for ritual conch shell
Cambodia, Khmer, Angkor period · 1100s · shell and bronze
Ritual conch shell
Cambodia, Khmer, Angkor Period, 12th-13th Century · 1100s · shell and bronze
Khmer Vase
Cambodia · 1100s · earthenware with dark brown glaze
Ritual conch shell base with dancing Hevajra
Cambodia, Khmer, Angkor Period, 12th-13th Century · 1100s · shell and bronze
Lion
Cambodia, Khmer, Angkorean period (877-1431) · 1100s · stone
Tripod stand with nagas for a ritual conch shell
Cambodia, Khmer, Angkor Period, 12th-13th Century · 1100s · shell and bronze
One of a Pair of Decorated Palanquin Hooks and Rings
Cambodia, Angkor Wat Style, 12th century · 1100–1175 · bronze
A Pair of Decorated Palanquin Hooks and Rings
Cambodia, Angkor Wat Style, 12th century · 1100–1175 · bronze
Pair of naga finials
Cambodia, probably Angkor · 1100s · bronze
Naga Finial
Cambodia, Angkor Wat Period, 12th century · 1100s · bronze
Head of Shiva
Cambodia, Angkor, 12th century · 1100s · sandstone
Sakyamuni Buddha
Burma, Pagan style, 12th century · 1100s · bronze
Earring
Cambodia, Angkorean Period (877-1431) · c. 1100s–1200s · bronze
Shiva
Cambodia, Angkor, Baphuon, early 12th century · early 1100s · gray sandstone
Naga-Enthroned Buddha
Cambodia, Angkor, Angkor Wat Period, 12th century · 1100s · bronze with traces of gilding
Shwezigon Pagoda
Pagan dynasty · 1102 · Bagan, Myanmar
A gilded cylindrical stupa that set the template for every later Burmese pagoda. King Anawrahta enshrined here the tooth of the Buddha brought from Sri Lanka — placed by an albino white elephant he released to choose the holy site.
Ananda Temple
Pagan Kingdom · 1105 · Myanmar
A cruciform white temple with four standing Buddhas, each eighteen feet tall. From a distance the gold umbrella on its stupa is the last thing to disappear.
Angkor Wat
Khmer Empire · 12th c. · Cambodia
The largest religious monument on earth. Built as a Hindu temple to Vishnu, converted to Buddhist use, and never fully abandoned.
Bagan
Pagan Kingdom · 11th–13th c. · Myanmar
More than 2,000 Buddhist temples and stupas on a plain by the Irrawaddy. At dawn hot air balloons drift over a landscape of brick towers.
Bayon
Khmer Empire · late 12th c. · Cambodia
The last great temple of Angkor — 200 serene faces carved into its towers, likely portraits of Jayavarman VII himself, watching from every direction.
Angkor Thom
Khmer Empire · late 12th c. · Cambodia
The last capital of the Khmer Empire — nine square kilometers walled in laterite. At its heart, the Bayon; at its gates, giants pulling serpents in cosmic tug-of-war.
Beng Mealea
Khmer · 12th c. · Cambodia
A temple in near-total collapse, largely untouched by restoration — stones thrown by fig-tree roots, cracks opening under the chapels. Angkor Wat as nature tried to reclaim it.
Angkor Wat Naga Palanquin Finial
Khmer · Angkor Wat period · 12th c. · bronze
A multi-headed cobra-king finial that crowned a palanquin pole — Vasuki coiling up to protect the canopy under which a noble rode through the temple courts.
Portable icon of Shakyamuni Buddha in the Earth-touching gesture
Cambodia, reign of Jayavarman 7th · late 1100s–early 1200s · Bronze
Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara
by Cambodia, possibly Ta Prohm of Tonle Bati, Ta Keo province
Cambodia · Southeast Asian · 1175–1225 · Sandstone
Head of a Male Deity (Deva)
by Cambodia, probably Angkor Thom
Cambodia · Southeast Asian · 1175–1225 · Sandstone
Frieze with Apsaras
Cambodia, Angkor, the Bayon, Reign of Jayavarman VII, late 12th-early 13th Century · late 1100s · sandstone
Palanquin Hook
Cambodia, probably Angkor · 1175–1230 · bronze
Banteay Chhmar
Khmer Empire · Jayavarman VII · 1181 · Cambodia
A vast jungle-wrapped temple 150 km from Angkor, its bas-reliefs showing battles against the Cham and faces of Avalokiteshvara staring out of ruined towers.
Ta Prohm
Khmer · Jayavarman VII · 1186 · Cambodia
The photogenic Angkor ruin, trees slowly pulling the stones apart. The French maintained it in the half-swallowed state in which they found it, as a contrast to the restored temples.
Colossal Head of a Deva
Cambodia, Angkor, Preah Khan · c. 1200 · sandstone
Preah Khan
Khmer Empire · Jayavarman VII · 1191 · Cambodia
A temple-monastery and university at Angkor, its halls so extensive they once housed 100,000 people. Left partly jungle-swallowed — silk-cotton tree roots grip the stonework.
Hevajra Bronze
Khmer · NE Thailand · c. 1200 · bronze
An eight-armed, four-headed dancing Hevajra — the esoteric Buddhist deity Jayavarman VII adopted when Angkor turned Mahayana in the twilight of the empire.
Flask
Thailand · 1200-1300
Female Head
by Indonesia
Eastern Java · Southeast Asian · 1201–1500 · Andesite
Javanese Kala-Mukha
Majapahit · 12th–14th c. · Java
"Face of Time" — a lion-demon face with bulging eyes and no lower jaw, carved over temple doorways to devour the evil that would pass through. Monstrous apotropaic face.
Pouring Vessel
Siam, Sawankhalok ware, late 13th-14th Century · late 1200s-1300s · stoneware
Female Figure
Eastern Java, Majapahit Dynasty (1295-1478) · 1293–1478 · terracotta
Female Torso
Eastern Java, Majapahit Dynasty (1293-1478) · Majapahit Dynasty (1293–1478) · terracotta
Sukhothai Historical Park
Sukhothai Kingdom · 13th–14th century · Thailand
Capital of the first Thai kingdom, whose 'walking Buddhas' introduced a uniquely flowing and androgynous figural type. The open ruins of Wat Si Chum still frame a 15-meter seated Buddha peering out from a narrow gap between high walls.
Head of a Woman
Java, Majapahit period (1294–1478) · 1300s–1400s · terracotta
Covered Jar, Sawankhalok ware
Thailand, Sukhothai Period, 14th century · 1300s · pottery
Covered Jar, Sawankhalok ware (lid)
Thailand, Sukhothai Period, 14th century · 1300s · pottery
Small Jar: Sawankhalok Ware
Thailand · 1300s–1400s · porcelain with celadon glaze
Storage Jar
Thailand, Sawankhalok, 14th century · 1300s · stoneware
Storage Jar: Blue-and-White Ware
Vietnam (Annam) · 1300s · porcelain with underglaze blue
Stem Cup with Dragon Decoration
Vietnam (Annam) · 1300s · porcelain with underglaze blue
Sawankhalok Bowl
by Thailand, possibly Sukhothai province
Thailand · 1301–1500 · Stoneware with celadon glaze
Sawankhalok Ware Covered Box
by Thailand, possibly Sukhothai province
Thailand · 1301–1500 · Glazed stoneware with iron-brown and blue-grey underglaze
Sawankhalok Ware Covered Box with Lotus Bud Knob
by Thailand, possibly Sukhothai province
Thailand · 1301–1500 · Glazed stoneware with iron-brown underglaze
Sukhothai
Sukhothai Kingdom · 13th–15th c. · Thailand
Serene standing Buddhas in the "walking" pose invented here — one hand raised, the other swinging at the hip, fabric flowing forward.
Besakih Temple
Balinese Hindu · 14th c. onward · Bali
The Mother Temple of Bali — 23 linked sanctuaries up the slope of Mount Agung. When Agung erupted in 1963, lava stopped meters from its gates.
Majapahit Piggy Bank
Majapahit · 14th c. · Java
A terracotta pig with a slot on its back for coins — excavated from Majapahit sites in the hundreds. The word "celengan" (piggy bank) is still Indonesian for a child's coin savings.
Sawankhalok Covered Jar
Sukhothai · 14th c. · Thailand
Glazed stoneware from the Sawankhalok kilns — a pear-shaped jar with incised and underglaze-iron decoration, its lid a little pagoda. Sukhothai-era ceramics were exported across Southeast Asia.
Cup
Vietnam · 1350-1450
Moai
Rapa Nui · 1250–1500 · Easter Island
Nearly 900 stone figures, carved and moved miles from the quarry. They face inland — watching over the villages, not out to sea.
Wat Phra That Doi Suthep
Lanna · 1383 · Chiang Mai
A mountaintop temple reached by a staircase of 306 steps flanked by mosaic-tiled nagas. Legend says King Nu Naone chose the site by tying a Buddha relic to a white elephant and following where it fell; it collapsed here and died.
Nan Madol
Saudeleur dynasty · 12th–17th c. · Pohnpei
A city of 92 artificial islets built from hexagonal basalt logs, laid across a lagoon. The "Venice of the Pacific" — and a place no one has fully explained.
Ahu and Moai of Easter Island
Rapa Nui · c. 1250–1500 CE · Rapa Nui
Ceremonial stone platforms on which the moai stood, facing inland over the village. A typical ahu runs 100 meters along the coast; some incorporate basalt pillars and red scoria pukao 'topknots' rolled from a separate quarry.
Buddha Head in Banyan Tree
Ayutthaya · 14th–18th c. · Thailand
A stone Buddha head trapped in the roots of a banyan tree at Wat Mahathat — severed when the Burmese sacked Ayutthaya in 1767, slowly embraced by the tree over centuries. Icon of ruin and regrowth.
Ayutthaya Gold Buddha
Ayutthaya · 14th c. · Thailand
A seated Buddha cast in pure gold over a resin core — found in a crypt at Wat Ratchaburana. Sealed into the stupa wall in 1424, unsealed in 1957 by thieves, rescued by police.
Bowl with a Conch Shell in the Center
by Vietnam
Vietnam · annamese · 1400–1499 · Glazed stoneware with cobalt-blue underglaze
Covered Bowl with Painted Decoration with Lid
Siam, Sawankhalok ware, 15th century · 1400s · stoneware
Covered Bowl with Painted Decoration (lid)
Siam, Sawankhalok ware, 15th century · 1400s · stoneware
Pottery Fragment
Siam, Sawankhalok ware, 15th century · 1400s · stoneware
Covered Bowl with Painted Decoration
Siam, Sawankhalok ware, 15th century · 1400s · stoneware
Ewer in the Shape of a Dragon
Vietnam (Annam) · 1400s · porcelain with underglaze blue
Double Ducks Jar
Vietnam (Annam) · 1400s · porcelain with underglaze blue and iron spots
Bird-Shaped Jar
Vietnam (Annam) · 1400s · porcelain with underglaze blue
Plaque with Buddha Triumphing over Mara
by Thailand
Thailand · Southeast Asian · 1401–1600 · Gold repoussé
Candi Sukuh
Majapahit · 15th c. · Java
A Hindu temple built as a truncated stone pyramid — closer to Maya than to Angkor. Its reliefs are frank about fertility; the statues at the gate tell you what they're for.

1500 – 1800

Polynesian Tiki
Polynesian · pre-contact · Pacific
Stone or wooden carvings of deified ancestors, dotting the Marquesas, Tahiti, and Hawaii. Variants spread as far as Easter Island's moai.
Marae
Polynesian · pre-European contact · central and eastern Pacific
Open-air rectangular ceremonial courtyards paved in coral rubble, with upright stone backrests for chiefs and priests. From Tahiti to Hawaii to Aotearoa, the marae was the meeting ground of cosmology, law, and genealogical recitation.
Rai Stones
Yapese · traditional · Micronesia
Massive limestone discs up to 4 meters across, quarried in Palau and shipped hundreds of sea miles to Yap. Used as currency — but too heavy to move; their ownership history is simply remembered.
Dish
Vietnam · 1500-1600
Bust of Crowned and Adorned Buddha
by Burma
Myanmar · Southeast Asian · 1501–1800 · Bronze
Ayutthaya
Ayutthaya Kingdom · 14th–18th c. · Thailand
The Thai capital before the Burmese sacked it in 1767. Brick prangs lean at odd angles around the old moated island.
Pura Tanah Lot
Balinese Hindu · 16th c. · Bali
A Hindu sea temple perched on a rock pounded by surf. At high tide it becomes an island; legend says sea snakes guard it from the base.
Wat Xieng Thong
Lan Xang · 1559–1560 · Luang Prabang
The royal temple of the Lao kingdom. Its sim has a sweeping tiered roof that nearly touches the ground; interior walls are covered in gilded-black lacquer tell-tales.
Tongkonan
Toraja · centuries old · Sulawesi, Indonesia
Ancestral houses with boat-shaped roofs soaring up at both ends. Each is built facing north, aligned to the cosmos of the Torajan dead.
Thiên Mụ Pagoda
Nguyễn lords · 1601 · Huế, Vietnam
Seven storeys of octagonal tower on the Perfume River. In 1963 a Thiên Mụ monk drove from here to Saigon and set himself on fire in protest — the photograph that shook a war.
Jeweled and Crowned Buddha (Jambupati)
by Burma
Myanmar · Southeast Asian · 1601–1700 · Bronze
Pura Ulun Danu Bratan
Balinese · 1633 · Indonesia
A multi-tiered meru shrine built on a small island in Lake Bratan, dedicated to Dewi Danu, the water goddess. Appears to float when the lake fills.
Tā Moko
Māori · pre-European onward · New Zealand
Tattoos chiseled (not punctured) into the skin, carrying genealogy and rank. Each design is unique to the person who wears it.
Hei-tiki
Māori · pre-European onward · New Zealand
Nephrite pendants of a human figure worn around the neck, passed down through generations. Each is named; each gains mana from the people who wear it.
Rongorongo
Rapa Nui · pre-contact? · Easter Island
Glyphs carved onto wooden tablets — one of the few independent writing systems in human history. Still undeciphered; most tablets were burned by missionaries.
Kris
Malay Archipelago · 13th c. onward
An asymmetric wavy dagger with nickel-iron pattern welding. Considered an heirloom with a spirit of its own — some can be named, inherited, or banned from leaving an island.
Paoay Church
Spanish colonial · 1710 · Ilocos Norte, Philippines
An 'earthquake Baroque' church with enormous coralline buttresses that curve outward like flying sleepers, bracing it against the region's seismic activity. Its bell tower stands detached in case of collapse.
Māori Wharenui
Aotearoa · 18th c. onward · New Zealand
A carved meeting house represents an ancestor's body — ridgepole as spine, rafters as ribs. Every panel tells a story in whakairo carving.
Vigan
Spanish colonial · 16th–19th century · Ilocos Sur, Philippines
A coastal trading town laid out on a grid before the 1572 Spanish conquest, blending Chinese, Ilocano, and Iberian elements into a distinct Philippine colonial vernacular. Calle Crisologo's cobbled streets and bahay-na-bato mansions survived Allied bombing because a Japanese general refused to burn it.
Māori Kapeu
Māori · 18th–early 19th c. · New Zealand
A curved pounamu (greenstone) ear ornament, worn by chiefs and passed down through generations. The curved form inherits the moon, the unborn fern, and the fish-hook.
Wat Phra Kaew
Rattanakosin · 1782 onward · Bangkok
The royal temple inside the Grand Palace, home to the Emerald Buddha — carved from a single block of jade, robed differently for each season.
Wat Pho
Rattanakosin · 1788 · Bangkok
Home of the 46-meter gold-leafed Reclining Buddha and Thailand's first public university, where medical texts were carved into marble plaques around the courtyard. Traditional Thai massage is still taught in an attached school founded in 1955.

1800 onward

Batik
Javanese · traditional · Indonesia
Patterns drawn in hot wax on cotton, then dyed — the wax resisting the color. Javanese court batiks reserved some motifs for royalty on pain of death.
Kūkaʻilimoku
Hawaiian · 18th–early 19th century · Hawaiʻi
Feathered figures of the war god of Kamehameha I, their faces sheathed in tens of thousands of red and yellow bird feathers meticulously tied to a wicker armature. Grimacing pearl-shell eyes and dog-tooth mouths dramatize the god's ferocity.
Barong Mask
Balinese · traditional · Indonesia
A fanged lion-dog costume, body of woven raffia, danced by two men. Barong fights the witch-queen Rangda in an endlessly restaged cosmic battle — good versus evil, never concluded.
Rumah Gadang
Minangkabau · traditional · Sumatra
"Big houses" with buffalo-horn roofs rising to upswept points. Matrilineal — owned by the women of the clan, extended and re-roofed as generations add on.
Mahiole
Hawaiian · 18th c.
A feather helmet, bright red and yellow, crested like the mohawk of a Greek warrior — worn by royal chiefs with their 'ahu'ula feather cloak. Rembrandt Peale painted one seen in Philadelphia around 1800.
Fijian Club
Fijian · 18th–19th c. · Fiji
A carved hardwood club for ceremonial and combat use — "ula" throwing clubs, "totokia" pineapple-headed clubs, each form named and categorized. Fijian clubs were family heirlooms.
Turban ornament
Java · 1800s · silver and topaz
Waist Cloth (Kain Panjeng)
Indonesia, Central Java, 19th century - early 20th century · 1800s-early 1900s · cotton: batik
Text, folio 4 (verso) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 6 (recto) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 14 (recto) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 7 (recto) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 5 (recto) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 9 (recto) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 11 (verso) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 12 (verso) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 13 (verso) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text and illumination, folio 16 (verso) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 9 (verso) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 10 (verso) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 3 (verso) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Top cover from a Kammavacha: Buddhist Ordination Text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Cover: lacquer, gilding, and glass appliqué on wood
Bowl
Melanesia, Papua New Guinea, Huon Gulf, Tami Islands, 19th century · 1800s · painted wood
Canoe Splashboard
Melanesia, Papua New Guinea, Massim Area, Trobriand Islands, 19th century · 1800s · wood
Ceremonial Adze
Polynesia, Mangaia, Cook Islands, 19th century · 1800s · wood, stone, fiber
Haft from a Ceremonial Adze
Polynesia, Mangaia, Cook Islands, 19th century · 1800s · wood, fiber
Blade from a Ceremonial Adze
Polynesia, Mangaia, Cook Islands, 19th century · 1800s · stone
Embroidered textile (piña)
Philippines · 1800s · embroidery in ecru thread on pina cloth
Fichu made of piña
Philippines · 1800s · piña cloth: ecru thread embroidery
Pendant (Hei-tiki)
Polynesia, New Zealand, Maori people, 19th century · 1800s · nephrite (greenstone)
Treasure Box (Wakahuia)
Polynesia, New Zealand, Maori people, 19th century · 1800s · wood with abalone shell
Fishing Canoe Prow
Polynesia, New Zealand, Maori people, 19th century · 1800s · wood with haliotis (pearl) shell inlay
Lace fragment
Philippines · 1800-1875
Text and illumination, folio 1 (verso) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
Text, folio 7 (verso) from a Kammavacha: Buddhist ordination text
Myanmar (Burma) · 1800s · Leaf: lacquer and gilding on cloth
War Club ('U'u)
Polynesia, Marquesas Islands, early 19th Century · early 1800s · stained ironwood
Stilt Footrest
Polynesia, Marquesas Islands, 19th century · early 1800s · wood
Gorget (Rei Miro)
Pacific Islands, Polynesia, Rapa Nui (Easter Island) · 1800s · wood
Ceremonial Hanging (Palepai)
by Paminggir people
Indonesia · indonesian · 1801–1900 · Cotton
Ceremonial Skirt (tapis)
by Abung people
Indonesia · indonesian · 1801–1825 · Two panels joined: cotton and silk
Woman's Ceremonial Skirt (Tapis)
by Abung
Indonesia · indonesian · 1801–1900 · Two panels joined: cotton and silk
Kneeling Monk
by Burma
Myanmar · Southeast Asian · 1801–1900 · Gilt bronze
Shoulder Cloth (Ragidup)
by Indonesia, Sumatra, Batak
Sumatra · indonesian · 1801–1900 · Three panels joined: side panels: cotton
Wat Arun
Rattanakosin · 1809 · Bangkok
The 'Temple of Dawn' across the Chao Phraya from Bangkok, its central prang encrusted with broken Chinese porcelain imported as ship ballast. Its five towers form a stone mandala of Mount Meru that Rama II rebuilt from a smaller Ayutthaya-era shrine.
Tongan 'Otua Fefine
Ha'apai · early 19th c. · Tonga
A small whale-ivory female deity figure — one of the rarest surviving Tongan carvings. Used in chiefly rites; most Tongan wood and ivory sculptures were destroyed at Christian conversion.
Hawaiian Feather Cloak
Hawaiian · 18th–19th c.
An 'ahu 'ula — thousands of tiny red and yellow feathers, knotted one by one onto netting over years, worn only by the highest chiefs. The yellow feathers had to be plucked from a bird that has perhaps one or two per body.
Cook Islands Staff God
Cook Islands · 18th–19th c.
A long wooden staff carved with a head at one end and a series of human figures reading down the shaft, once wrapped in bark cloth and feathers — the condensed ancestry of a chiefly line.
Two Panels from a Skirt
by Philippines
Philippines · 1825–1835 · Pineapple fiber
Ceremonial Cloth (tampan)
by Paminggir people
Indonesia · indonesian · 1825–1875 · Cotton
Māori Hei-tiki
Māori · 19th c. · New Zealand
A greenstone pendant in the form of a squatting ancestor, head tilted, hands on hips. Worn around the neck, passed down generations — each tiki accumulates the mana of its wearers.
Imperial City of Huế
Nguyễn dynasty · 1802–1945 · Vietnam
The Vietnamese Forbidden City. Yellow-glazed gates, moats, throne halls, all pointed at the Perfume River. Much destroyed in the 1968 Tet Offensive; much slowly being rebuilt.
Waka (Māori War Canoe)
Māori · 18th–19th c. · Aotearoa New Zealand
Large war canoes carved from a single tōtara trunk. Prows and sternposts carved with ancestors; the hull painted ochre. Rowed by eighty paddlers, two drummers, a chief at the stern.
Piña cloth
Philippines · mid-1800s · piña fiber, silk
Short Club (Wahaika)
Polynesia, New Zealand, Maori people, mid-19th Century · mid 1800s · wood
Wayang Golek
Sundanese · 17th c. onward · West Java
Carved wooden rod puppets, fully dressed in miniature batik. Performed in all-night epics of the Ramayana and Mahabharata — the dalang puppeteer plays every voice.
Tau Tau
Toraja · Sulawesi, Indonesia
Carved wooden effigies of the dead, placed in cliff-face galleries above Toraja villages. Each tau tau faces outward and is redressed periodically by descendants; some are centuries old.
Okir Carving
Maranao · 18th century onward · Mindanao, Philippines
Curvilinear vegetal ornament and geometric naga motifs carved into wooden house beams, brass betel boxes, and the okir frontispiece of the Torogan. The tradition survives Spanish and American colonial pressure as a distinct non-Christian Filipino art.
Nguzunguzu
Solomon Islands · 19th c.
A carved black canoe prow with white shell inlay — a protective head lashed to the front of a war canoe, meant to ward off water-spirits that cause shipwreck.
Marquesan Tiki
Marquesan · 19th c. · Polynesia
Stocky ancestor figures of stone and wood, great round eyes, planted hands on rounded bellies. Polynesian ancestry made visible; the form Gauguin spent his last years sketching.
Māori Pare (Door Lintel)
Māori · 19th c. · New Zealand
A carved and painted wooden door lintel for a meeting house — three figures, spiral chests, tongues protruding. The pare marks the threshold between the everyday and the sacred inside.
Burmese Kammavacha
Burmese Buddhist · 19th c. · Myanmar
An illuminated palm-leaf or lacquered manuscript containing the ordination text — the words a novice repeats to become a monk. Folios bound at top, dense with gold script.
Papuan Ancestral Figure
Melanesian · 19th c. · Papua New Guinea
A standing wooden ancestor with an exaggerated phallus, angular face, arms at the sides. Ancestral figures stood inside spirit houses to watch over clan descendants.
Crowned and Bejewelled Buddha Seated on an Elephant Throne
by Burma
Myanmar · Southeast Asian · 1850–1900 · Gilded and lacquered wood with paint and colored glass
Shrine
Burma · 1850-1870
Shadow puppet
Java · 1850-1880
Knife (Mandau) and scabbard
Indonesia, Borneo · c. 1900 · metal, wood, bone, hair, and braided cloth cord
Phra Pathom Chedi
Mon → Rama IV · 3rd c. BCE core, 1850s outer · Thailand
The tallest stupa in the world — 120 meters of orange-tiled bell. A Mon-era chedi encased in a 19th-century Siamese one, as if a building were its own tomb.
Colossal Statue of the Gautama close to the N.end of the wooden bridge
by Linnaeus Tripe
Burma · August 1855 to November 1855
Pali grammar
Burma · 1858
Tomika Te Mutu, chief of Ngāiterangi
by Crombie, John Nicol
New Zealand · June 1860
Karoana
by Peyman, Benjamin
New Zealand · ca.1860
Buddha in Tavatimsa Heaven
Burma · before 1865
Photograph
by Kerr, Henry
Solomon Islands · 1865
Photographic portrait of two Maori girls
New Zealand · c 1865
Eruera Maihi Patuone
New Zealand · c 1865
Nesting box set
Burma · before 1867
Saung gauk
Burma · before 1869
Torogan House
Maranao · 19th century · Lanao, Philippines
An enormous elevated wooden house for the sultan's family, its floor held aloft by two-meter carved tree trunks whose projecting panolong prows evoke ships. Inside, one single gigantic room divides only by textile hangings and movable panels.
Portrait of Pare Watene
by Foy Brothers
New Zealand · ca.1871-1909
Buddha
Burma · before 1874
Dagger
Melanesia, Papua New Guinea, Sepik River region, 19th century · c. 1880 · cassowary bone
Sleeve
Philippines · c. 1875–1900 · plain weave piña cloth with embroidery
Blouse front
Philippines · c. 1875–1900 · plain weave piña cloth with embroidery
Malangan Carvings
New Ireland · 19th c. onward · Papua New Guinea
Elaborately painted wooden figures carved for a single memorial ceremony, then deliberately destroyed or allowed to rot. Their knowledge was owned as intellectual property; only certain carvers could make certain forms.
Bulul
Ifugao · 19th century · northern Luzon, Philippines
Carved wooden rice-granary guardians placed in pairs on sacks of grain and anointed annually with pig's blood and rice wine. Ifugao priests chant lineages of ancestors while invoking each Bulul; the more darkened with offerings, the more powerful.
Headdress
Burma · before 1880
Tray
Burma · before 1880
Marshall Islands Stick Chart
Marshallese · traditional · Micronesia
A lattice of palm ribs and shells describing ocean swells — not land maps but wave-maps, for navigating between atolls too small to see from far. Navigators memorized them, then left them behind.
Tubular Skirt (Sarong)
by Indonesia, Java, North Coast, Pekalongan
Java · indonesian · 1890–1910 · Cotton
Femme Cueillant des Fruits et Oviri
by Gauguin, Paul
Tahiti · April 1891 - September 1893
Asmat Carving
Asmat people · Papua, Indonesia
Bisj poles and shields carved from mangrove with sharpened bone. Each pole commemorates a death and demands it be avenged.
Mentawai Body Tattoo
Mentawai people · millennia · Indonesia
A Mentawai sikerei (shaman) in full body tattoo. Designs carry rank and history; the practice is said to be one of the oldest continuous tattoo traditions on earth.
Thai Silk
Thai · 13th c. onward
Hand-reeled mulberry silk dyed and woven into patterned sashes, sarongs, and court textiles. Nearly extinct by 1945; an American expatriate named Jim Thompson revived the industry before vanishing in Malaysia.
Hmong Textile
Hmong · traditional · SE Asia
Reverse-appliqué and cross-stitch panels in jewel colors — "flower cloth" (paj ntaub). Often incorporates figures and stories; since the 1970s used to document Hmong migration and war.
Tapa Cloth
Polynesian bark cloth · pre-European to present · Fiji/Tonga/Samoa
Non-woven fabric beaten from paper-mulberry bark and stamped, painted, or smoked with fine geometric patterns. Each region of Polynesia developed its own grammar; Fijian gatu vakaviti like this one are made for weddings and funeral exchanges.
Malanggan Carvings
New Ireland · 19th–20th century · Papua New Guinea
Painted wooden figures made for a single memorial ceremony and then deliberately allowed to rot or be sold off. European museums hold hundreds of examples; the meaning of each belonged solely to the man who commissioned it and died with the performance.
Vanuatu Slit Drum
Ni-Vanuatu · traditional
A vertical log drum carved with a face, slit up the side, played with wooden mallets. Group drumming calls up ancestral spirits; solo drumming calls the village from the fields.
Mandaya Dagmay Ikat
Mandaya · Mindanao · early 20th c. · hemp, warp-ikat
A hemp dagmay cloth woven by the Mandaya of southern Mindanao — the warp-ikat patterns encode ancestor-spirit figures, and a woman's weaving skill shaped her marriage prospects.
Wall Hanging or Ceiling Canopy
Cambodia, Khmer People, early 20th century · early 1900s · tabby weave, weft ikat; silk
Yam Mask
Melanesia, Papua New Guinea, Abelam People, 20th century · 1900s · pigment, basketry
Tapa Cloth Beater (l'e kuku)
Polynesia, Hawaii, 20th century · before 1930 · wood
Textile fragment (Binakol)
Philippines, Ilokos, Norte Province, Luzon, 20th century(?) · c. early 1900s(?) · woven cotton; extended tabby weave
Mandaya textile (composite dagmay)
Philippines, Mindanao · c. early 1900s · plain weave hemp, dyed
Mandaya ikat textile (dagmay)
Philippines, Mindanao · c. early 1900s · hemp: plain weave, dyed
Abacá lace sampler
Philippines · c. 1910–23 · abaca fiber(?): knitting
Backpack (Bagobo people)
Philippines · early 1900s · Handwoven abacá cloth, printed cotton cloth, abacá fiber (braided/plaited cordage), glass beads, metal sequins, metal rings, fiber tassels
Headcloth (Yakan seputangan)
Philippines · c. early 1900s · plain weave with paired weft: cotton; supplementary weft: silk
Skirt
Philippines · 1900-1995
Hinggi
Indonesia, Sumba, early 20th century · early 1900s · cotton, ikat dyed
Scripture box and shrine
Burma · ca. 1900
Infant's Carrying Cloth, or Man's Ceremonial Headgear
Indonesia, North Sumatra, Toba Batak People, early 20th century · early 1900s · warp-faced tabby with areas of ikat
Pua (Ceremonial Blanket or Hanging)
Borneo, Kalimantan, Iban Tribe, 20th century · 1900–1970 · tabby weave, warp ikat; cotton
Woman's Sash
Oceania, Micronesia, Caroline Islands, Early 20th century (?) · c 1900s ? · dyed plantfiber; plain weave, twill weave and floats
Whistler no. 2
by Mortimer Luddington Menpes
Australia · 1902–1903 · Drypoint in black with plate tone on cream laid paper
Whistler no. 3
by Mortimer Luddington Menpes
Australia · 1902–1903 · Etching and drypoint in black with plate tone on cream wove paper
Bisj Pole
Asmat · 19th–20th c. · West Papua
A mangrove tree stood on its head and carved into ancestral figures and phallic projections — raised at funeral feasts to demand vengeance for the dead. Left to rot after the rite.
Papua Spirit Board
Papuan Gulf · early 20th c. · Papua New Guinea
A flat wooden board (gope) incised with a clan ancestor face — hung in a men's house for protection. Each board had its own spirit, its own name.
Abelam Baba Mask
Abelam · 20th century · Sepik, Papua New Guinea
Spirit masks used in yam-harvest ceremonies celebrating the long ceremonial yams that Abelam men cultivate for years. The masks top painted ceremonial houses up to thirty meters tall, decorated with rows of ancestor faces in red, black, and white.
Abelam Yam Mask
Abelam · 20th c. · Papua New Guinea
A basketry mask worn by the largest yam of the harvest — the Abelam rank yams by length (often three meters), dress them in individual regalia, and rank them in a nuanced hierarchy of masculinity.
Shield
Australia · 1950 - 1955
Shoulder cloth
Sumatra · 1950-1959
Tiwi Pukumani Poles
Tiwi · traditional · Australia
Tall painted logs planted around a grave for the Pukumani death ceremony — ochre-painted, individually carved, left to weather and eventually return to the earth.
Textile
Philippines · 1963-1970
Angkor Wat, Cambodia
by Burrows, Larry
Cambodia · 1968
Doll
New Zealand · 1975
Trade cloth
Indonesia · ca. 1982
Papunya Tula Painting
Western Desert · from 1971 · Australia
Acrylic on board by the painters of Papunya — Warlpiri, Anmatyerre, Luritja artists encoding Dreaming stories in dot patterns that read as maps, as abstractions, as secrets only partially revealed.
Two men sitting at a waterhole
by Pike, Jimmy
Melbourne (Australia) · 1989
Wat Rong Khun (White Temple)
by Chalermchai Kositpipat
Contemporary Thai Buddhist · Chalermchai Kositpipat · 1997 onward · Chiang Rai
An entire temple sculpted white, glittering with shards of mirror. The artist intends it to take centuries to complete; hands rise from hell at the entrance.
Poetry of a Drought
by Bot, G.W.
Australia · 1997
Banaue Rice Terraces
Ifugao · 2000+ years old · Philippines
Stone-walled terraces climbing the mountainside. Built without wheeled carts, draft animals, or cement. If unwrapped they'd circle half the globe.
Gelam Nguzu Kazi
by Bosun, David
Torres Strait Islands · 2001
Ap Au Aidal
by Missi, Billy
Torres Strait Islands · 2001
Kaningarra
by Bootja Bootja Napangarti, Susie
Australia · 2002
Down Under
by Cotton, Shane
New Zealand · 2004
←East Asia Americas→
